blocks.speaker: Crystal
blocks.content: I know. That's why this is so hard.

blocks.speaker: Crystal
blocks.content: Larry...
A long pause. Long enough that I thought I'd said the wrong thing.

blocks.speaker: Crystal
blocks.content: I don't know.

blocks.speaker: Crystal
blocks.content: You don't have to. I just needed you to know.

blocks.speaker: Crystal
blocks.content: Thank you. That's — thank you.
We stayed on the phone after that. Neither of us said much. There wasn't much to say — we'd both already said the real things. But neither of us hung up.
I could hear her settling. The breathing evening out. Somewhere on her end a light went on inside the house — her parents, probably — and I could hear the sound change slightly through the door.

blocks.speaker: Crystal
blocks.content: I should go back in. My mom's been patient.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: Okay.

blocks.speaker: Crystal
blocks.content: I'm glad I called.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: Me too.
A pause. Neither of us moved to end it.

blocks.speaker: Crystal
blocks.content: Goodnight, Larry.

blocks.speaker: Larry
blocks.content: Goodnight.
The line went quiet.
I held the phone and didn't put it down for a long time. I just sat there in the dark with it. My jacket still on. My shoes still on.

She was sitting on the front steps of a house she didn't know yet, in a city she arrived in today, in a neighborhood whose sounds she hadn't learned yet. And the first moment she had alone she called me.

She asked Jonathan about me. She took the long way to class. She shifted her fingers so they fit right and then three days later she was in Atlanta saying my heart melted every time you looked at me from a porch she'd never sat on before.
I don't know when it happened. I wasn't deciding to. I just noticed at some point that my face was wet.
I didn't wipe it. I just sat there and let it happen in the dark where nobody could see it. Larry Jr. was asleep. The apartment was quiet. There was nobody to hold it together for.

It wasn't loud. It wasn't dramatic. It was just — something releasing that had been pressed down since Friday morning. The empty locker and the game and the nod with Jonathan and the walk home and all of it. And underneath all of it, her voice on those steps saying things nobody had ever said to me before.
It passed. Eventually it passed.
I sat in the quiet for a while after. Then I took my shoes off. Then I lay down on top of the covers in my jacket and stared at the ceiling.
I didn't sleep.
